header: 3SeePhys Pro: A Fine-Grained Benchmark for Modality-Transfer

images.caption: Figure 1:Overview of the four modality-transfer levels inSeePhys Pro.Each seed problem is transformed into four semantically aligned variants that progressively move problem-critical information from language to vision: Level 1 is text-only, Level 2 moves structure into the image, Level 3 further moves variables and labels into the image, and Level 4 renders the full problem into a single visual input.

header: 5Training-Time Diagnostic: Can RL Help Close the Modality Gap?

images.caption: Figure 3:RL does not appear to close modality gaps.Qwen3-VL-4B is trained on source-matched but test-disjoint vision-necessary physics data and evaluated on unmaskedSeePhys ProLevel 1–4. The top row tracks validation accuracy on each modality-transfer level after normal and blind RL updates; the bottom row tracks the total transfer gapΔT=A1−A4\Delta_{\mathrm{T}}=A_{1}-A_{4}and variable-grounding gapΔV=A2−A3\Delta_{\mathrm{V}}=A_{2}-A_{3}. Both normal and blind RL improve validation accuracy, but the visually induced gaps remain large.

Figure 4:Blind gains are not unique toSeePhys Pro.Peak validation gains for normal and blind RL on external physics and math benchmarks. Blind RL often recovers a substantial fraction of normal RL gains, and on several math settings it matches or exceeds normal RL.

header: 6Mechanism Analysis: What Does Blind RL Learn?

images.caption: Figure 5:Mechanism controls for blind-training gains.Text deletion, targeted deletion, mask-rate ablation, and post-format-saturation controls show that blind gains depend on residual textual/distributional cues rather than valid visual evidence. All gains are computed on unmasked validation sets.

Figure 6:Data pipeline for constructingSeePhys Pro.We describe the construction process as four logical stages: source collection, curation, transformation, and controlled sketching into raw, structural, and variable-level variants.

Figure 7:SeePhys Proand MathVerse Data EmbeddingsPanels (a, b) are the embeddings of text and multimodal inputs at Level 2 and Level 3 ofSeePhys Pro. Panels (c, d) are the embeddings of text and multimodal inputs for the Vision Intensive and Vision Dominant subsets of MathVerse.

Figure 8:PhysRL-38K training-pool validation with Qwen3-VL-4B.We train with GSPO on PhysRL-38K using two sequence-length settings and evaluate on held-out, unmasked validation sets. Curves show that validation accuracy improves on SeePhys Pro Level 3/4 and PhysReason, while training reward accuracy also increases. Duplicate values from interrupted/resumed runs are averaged by step before smoothing for display.

Figure 9:Post-format-saturation gains.After the format reward crosses90%90\%, answer accuracy can still increase substantially, especially in the Qwen2.5-VL-7B math runs highlighted in the main paper. This helps rule out a purely format-compliance explanation for blind gains.

Figure 10:Full image-mask-rate ablation curves.We plot accuracy and format reward for Qwen3-VL-4B trained with different image mask rates and evaluated on unmasked SeePhys Pro and PhysReason. Main-paper Figure5summarizes the peak gains within the first 200 updates.
Figure 11:Separate cross-benchmark peak-gain plots.These are expanded views of Figure4.
